title: Math Rule Broken by Donut Shapes
summary: For over 150 years, a math rule by Pierre Ossian Bonnet said that if you know two things about a surface—how distances work and how it bends—you can figure out its exact shape. But now, mathematicians from three universities have found a surprise. They made two donut-shaped surfaces, called tori, that have the same distances and bending at every point, but the shapes are different. This breaks the old rule. It shows that even with all the local details, you can't always know the whole shape. The discovery solves a problem that people have been trying to solve for decades.

a surface that looks like a ring or a donut, also called a torus

background_read: Geometry is the study of shapes and spaces. For a long time, mathematicians thought that if you knew how distances work and how a surface bends at every point, you could figure out its exact shape. This idea came from a French mathematician named Pierre Ossian Bonnet over 150 years ago. Surfaces can be things like spheres, planes, or donut shapes called tori. The new discovery shows that even with all this information, two donut shapes can look the same locally but be different overall, breaking the old rule and helping solve a big math problem.
Article_Structure: WHO: Mathematicians from Technical University of Munich, Technical University of Berlin, and North Carolina State University
WHAT: Found two donut-shaped surfaces that break a 150-year-old math rule by having the same distances and bending but different shapes
WHERE: In geometry research at universities
WHY: To show that local details don't always determine the whole shape, solving a decades-old problem

perspectives.description: This is an exciting breakthrough that changes how we understand surfaces in geometry.
